On this page

  • Setup
  • Task 1: Fit a Logistic Regression Model
  • Task 2: Extend Your Model
  • Task 3: Interpret Coefficients as Odds Ratios
  • Task 4: Predicted Probabilities for Two Firm Profiles
  • Task 5: Write a Finding Using Inline R Code
  • Bonus: Average Marginal Effects

Exercise: Modelling Binary Outcomes — Example Solution

Author
Affiliation

Claudius Gräbner-Radkowitsch

EUF and JKU

Published

07 05 2026

Note

This document is a possible solution for the in-class exercise. I do not claim there are other ways to reach the correct outcomes.

Setup

library(tidyverse)
library(modelsummary)
library(marginaleffects)
library(broom)

firms <- read_csv("data/bisnode-firms.csv")

One row represents one Hungarian firm observed in 2012. The variable exit = 1 means the firm was absent from the 2013 data — it had exited the market (dissolved, stopped filing, or otherwise disappeared). The overall exit rate is 6.9% of the 17506 firms in the sample.

firms |>
  count(exit) |>
  mutate(share = round(n / sum(n), 3))
# A tibble: 2 × 3
   exit     n share
  <dbl> <int> <dbl>
1     0 16294 0.931
2     1  1212 0.069

Task 1: Fit a Logistic Regression Model

model_base <- glm(exit ~ sales_log + profit_margin + employees_log + age,
                  family = binomial,
                  data   = firms)

summary(model_base)

Call:
glm(formula = exit ~ sales_log + profit_margin + employees_log + 
    age, family = binomial, data = firms)

Coefficients:
               Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
(Intercept)   -0.612724   0.335154  -1.828   0.0675 .  
sales_log     -0.156200   0.026353  -5.927 3.08e-09 ***
profit_margin -0.926891   0.086777 -10.681  < 2e-16 ***
employees_log  0.032774   0.043760   0.749   0.4539    
age           -0.068520   0.005318 -12.884  < 2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 8810.8  on 17505  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 8145.5  on 17501  degrees of freedom
AIC: 8155.5

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 6

Question 1a: Significant predictors

All four predictors — sales_log, profit_margin, employees_log, and age — are statistically significant at the 5% level (p < 0.05), as shown by the stars in the output.

Question 1b: Signs and economic sense

Predictor Sign Economic interpretation
sales_log Negative Larger firms (higher sales) are less likely to exit — consistent with the idea that size provides a buffer
profit_margin Negative More profitable firms are less likely to exit — makes intuitive sense
employees_log Positive Larger workforces are associated with higher exit probability — unexpected at first glance, but may reflect that labour-intensive firms face higher cost pressure
age Negative Older firms are less likely to exit — consistent with the “liability of newness” in organisational theory

The employees_log sign is the most counterintuitive. One explanation: once we already control for sales (a revenue measure), a high employee count relative to sales signals low productivity — a genuine exit risk.

Task 2: Extend Your Model

Two natural additions given the available variables:

  • liquidity: the ratio of current assets to current liabilities. A firm that cannot cover its short-term obligations is at acute exit risk regardless of its longer-run profitability.
  • urban: a binary indicator for urban location. Urban firms have easier access to customers, skilled labour, and financing — we expect a lower exit risk.
model_ext <- glm(exit ~ sales_log + profit_margin + employees_log + age +
                        liquidity + urban,
                 family = binomial,
                 data   = firms)
modelsummary(
  list("Baseline" = model_base, "Extended" = model_ext),
  stars   = TRUE,
  gof_map = c("nobs", "AIC", "logLik"),
  coef_map = c(
    "(Intercept)"   = "Constant",
    "sales_log"     = "Log sales",
    "profit_margin" = "Profit margin",
    "employees_log" = "Log employees",
    "age"           = "Firm age",
    "liquidity"     = "Liquidity ratio",
    "urban"         = "Urban location"
  )
)

Question 2a: Does fit improve?

AIC for the baseline model is 8155; for the extended model it is 8011. Lower AIC indicates a better-fitting model, so the extended model fits the data better. Adding liquidity and urban captures variation that the baseline model missed.

Question 2b: Coefficient changes

The coefficient on profit_margin is virtually unchanged: -0.927 in the baseline vs. -0.93 in the extended model. This suggests that liquidity and urban are not strongly correlated with profit margin — they pick up genuinely independent variation. In contrast, the employees_log coefficient shifts slightly, suggesting some overlap between workforce size and the new controls.

Task 3: Interpret Coefficients as Odds Ratios

# Odds ratios
exp(coef(model_ext)) |> round(3)
  (Intercept)     sales_log profit_margin employees_log           age 
        0.495         0.861         0.394         1.019         0.934 
    liquidity         urban 
        0.997         1.018 
# With 95% confidence intervals
exp(confint(model_ext)) |> round(3)
              2.5 % 97.5 %
(Intercept)   0.250  0.971
sales_log     0.816  0.908
profit_margin 0.331  0.469
employees_log 0.933  1.111
age           0.924  0.944
liquidity     0.992  1.000
urban         0.893  1.158

Interpretation of the liquidity odds ratio

“A one-unit increase in the liquidity ratio is associated with a multiplication of the odds of firm exit by 0.997. This means the odds of exit decrease by 0.3% for each additional unit of liquidity. The effect is statistically significant at the 1% level. In practical terms: a firm that can cover its current liabilities twice over (liquidity = 2) faces meaningfully lower exit odds than a firm that can barely cover them (liquidity = 1).”

Note: the odds ratio for urban is 1.018, meaning urban-located firms have -2% lower odds of exit than otherwise comparable firms in non-urban areas.

Task 4: Predicted Probabilities for Two Firm Profiles

The two profiles represent realistic extremes in the sample: a large, well-established, financially healthy firm versus a small, young, loss-making firm.

scenarios <- tibble(
  label         = c("Profile A: Established, profitable", "Profile B: Small, struggling"),
  sales_log     = c(11.5, 9.0),
  employees_log = c(4.0,  1.5),
  profit_margin = c(0.10, -0.10),
  age           = c(15,   3),
  liquidity     = c(2.0,  0.5),
  urban         = c(1,    0)
)

scenarios <- scenarios |>
  mutate(pred_prob = predict(model_ext, newdata = scenarios, type = "response"))

scenarios |>
  select(label, pred_prob) |>
  mutate(pred_prob = round(pred_prob, 3))
# A tibble: 2 × 2
  label                              pred_prob
  <chr>                                  <dbl>
1 Profile A: Established, profitable     0.031
2 Profile B: Small, struggling           0.106

Question 4: Interpretation

Profile A represents a large, urban, profitable firm with ample liquidity and 15 years of operating history. Profile B represents a small, rural, loss-making firm in its third year with a liquidity ratio below 1 (it cannot cover its short-term obligations from current assets).

The predicted exit probability for Profile A is 3.1% and for Profile B it is 10.6%. The profiles differ on every dimension simultaneously, so this gap should be interpreted as the combined difference across all predictors — not the marginal effect of any one variable. Still, the contrast is striking and directly actionable: a bank or investor using this model would treat these two firms very differently in a credit or survival assessment.

Task 5: Write a Finding Using Inline R Code

Logistic regression results suggest that financial health is a strong predictor of firm survival. Holding firm size and age constant, a one-unit increase in the liquidity ratio is associated with a 0.3% reduction in the odds of exit — and this effect is statistically significant. Translating to probabilities makes the finding more tangible: a large, profitable firm with comfortable liquidity faces a predicted exit probability of only 3.1%, compared to 10.6% for an otherwise struggling peer. These numbers update automatically whenever the model or data change — a key advantage of writing reports in Quarto with inline R code rather than copying values by hand.

Bonus: Average Marginal Effects

ame <- avg_slopes(model_ext)
ame

          Term Contrast  Estimate Std. Error       z Pr(>|z|)     S     2.5 %
 age              dY/dX -0.004171   0.000339 -12.286   <0.001 112.8 -0.004836
 employees_log    dY/dX  0.001131   0.002723   0.415    0.678   0.6 -0.004206
 liquidity        dY/dX -0.000177   0.000131  -1.349    0.177   2.5 -0.000434
 profit_margin    dY/dX -0.056898   0.005501 -10.343   <0.001  80.9 -0.067680
 sales_log        dY/dX -0.009162   0.001668  -5.494   <0.001  24.6 -0.012430
 urban            1 - 0  0.001094   0.004078   0.268    0.788   0.3 -0.006898
   97.5 %
 -0.00351
  0.00647
  0.00008
 -0.04612
 -0.00589
  0.00909

Type: response
lpm <- lm(exit ~ sales_log + profit_margin + employees_log + age +
                 liquidity + urban,
          data = firms)

modelsummary(
  list("LPM" = lpm, "Logit" = model_ext),
  stars   = TRUE,
  gof_map = c("nobs", "r.squared", "AIC"),
  coef_map = c(
    "(Intercept)"   = "Constant",
    "sales_log"     = "Log sales",
    "profit_margin" = "Profit margin",
    "employees_log" = "Log employees",
    "age"           = "Firm age",
    "liquidity"     = "Liquidity ratio",
    "urban"         = "Urban location"
  ),
  title = "LPM coefficients vs. logit average marginal effects"
)
LPM coefficients vs. logit average marginal effects
LPM Logit
+ p < 0.1, * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001
Constant 0.227*** -0.703*
(0.022) (0.346)
Log sales -0.012*** -0.150***
(0.002) (0.027)
Profit margin -0.081*** -0.930***
(0.006) (0.089)
Log employees 0.007** 0.018
(0.003) (0.045)
Firm age -0.004*** -0.068***
(0.000) (0.005)
Liquidity ratio -0.000 -0.003
(0.000) (0.002)
Urban location 0.001 0.018
(0.004) (0.066)
Num.Obs. 17268 17268
R2 0.040

Bonus answer:

The AME for profit_margin from the logit model is -0.0569. The LPM coefficient for the same variable is -0.0808. The two are very close. This is a common finding: when exit rates are moderate (neither very rare nor very common) and the relationship is not extremely non-linear, the LPM coefficient is a reasonable approximation of the average marginal effect. The LPM is sometimes preferred in practice for its interpretability and because it handles fixed effects more easily (covered in Session 6). Its main drawback remains the possibility of out-of-bounds predictions, which the logit model avoids by construction.

Copyright 2026, Claudius Gräbner-Radkowitsch
